data.title: Visa: Not Cheap Compounder With The DOJ Highlighting Its Moat
data.description:
Visa (NYSE:V) is an entrenched payment network that produces gobs of cash flow with 40% cash flow margin. Revenue is tied to payment flows around the world which has allowed them to be a beneficiary of inflation the past 3 years.

I wrote about Visa by in 2019 discussing its moat. The business has a wide moat and is known quite well among quality investors. This is easy to see through some basic financial metrics:

  • Return on Equity 49%
  • Return on Capital 25%
  • EBITDA Margin 70%
  • Income conversion to cash flow >100%

All these attractive financial metrics are produced because the company has a durable network effect. The network effects have been molded and endured much competition over the years as the fintech industry has ramped up. Having built the toll-road between the account holders and merchants, they've been able to collect a small percentage of every cashless transaction using a Visa card.

Even recently with the DOJ's lawsuit, the document for part of it sounds like a sell-side equity analysis:

The Story

Visa is one of the most durable and profitable businesses I know. So, how did this occur? I wouldn't start by looking at the balance sheet, as that doesn't really highlight what has transpired over time. The focus has been on growing the network effects and continuing to direct/grow payment transactions on its rails. Which basically means building the VisaNet platform. The way this occurred is by being one of the most trusted networks for financial transactions and was created through marketing. Visa has been running their marketing spend around $1 billion annually. This is a substantial amount of dollars when compared to their overall expenses.

In addition, the lawsuit also highlights Visa leveraging its monopoly like position to create routing deals, where Visa leverages its non-contestable transactions they help with and create contracts for most of the transaction volume to go through their rails. This has benefited them greatly in reducing competition after the Durbin Amendment was introduced to help spur competition among debit card transactions.

Digital wallets were another hurdle for Visa years ago, as this setup could possibly bypass Visa's rails all together. Providers of digital wallets wanted the consumer to link directly to their bank accounts and transact in a closed loop, leaving Visa out of the picture. Customers complained about not having the ability to link their Visa card to the digital wallet, and once one digital wallet provider gave in, many had to follow. Consumers trust Visa, it's accepted at many places around the world, and they want their rewards. A digital wallet provider that didn't allow Visa as a payment option was at a disadvantage to ones that did.

Cash Flow and Valuation

As a cash flow investor, the higher the yield the more attractive the investment is. As you start to look at higher quality companies it's a little more difficult to judge if the cash flow yield shows if the organization is undervalued as quality tends to have lower yields. Below is a good visual of the yield trend over the last 5 years.

Based on looking at this chart, anything above a 4% yield would have been an opportune time to purchase some shares. Buying at a favorable valuation, investors get a possible rerating benefit of the yield trending back to the average, along with the growth of the cash flow. The FCF has basically doubled since 2020:

With being a capital light business, free cash flow margins are high and allow management to return cash to investors efficiently. I say efficiently, as the company is still able to grow FCF and continue to return cash at a substantial rate. Over the last 10 years, Visa has reduced their shares outstanding by 20% with paying a modest dividend along the way.

Path for Continued Growth

Growth will continue to come from transaction volume and fortifying the network. Transaction volume comes from a strong consumer and adding more card users. Total cards continue to consistently grow per the most recent quarterly data:

Along with payment volume as that was also mid to high single digit growth this past quarter.

Here are some key financial metrics that give a good summary of 2023 operations.

I would like to point out that in 2023 payment volume was $12.3T on Visa's rails. For comparison, back in 2017 payment volume was $10.3T. Two trillion dollars more was funnelled to Visa's rails over this 6-year period.

Risk

Regulatory risk is real, and that's obvious with the DOJ lawsuit. This lawsuit will likely take years to come to any sort of conclusion, but highlights Visa is not immune to regulatory issues.

Other forms of payment methods like crypto are risks. This risk though seems low and futuristic as behavioral changes would have to be made as many consumers use their card or phone for payment in the physical world.

Upcoming Quarter

The 4th quarter and full-year earnings call will be October 29th. As I don't put too much emphasis on what's going to happen in the next earnings quarter, I would like to highlight full-year outlook is supposed to see low double-digit EPS growth on the high end. Good information and data is acquired on these calls to form a future outlook long-term, and any Visa investor should review the information and data put out next week. I suspect more will be discussed also about the DOJ lawsuit and future implications.

Final Thoughts

Visa is a compounder and has been for decades. High returns on capital, low capital intensity, and organic growth have all been contributors to producing its outsized returns for investors. Management has been protecting their network effects with everything they have, as owning the toll road on cashless transactions around the world is a priceless asset. For the time being, valuation looks a little stretched from a FCF yield perspective, especially when compared to risk-free rates, as I consider this a hold for now.

Time will tell with what the DOJ lawsuit brings, but the report does highlight some monopolistic dynamics. One can argue though many players have come into the space over the last 5 years creating more competition and Visa has had to navigate through that. I believe Visa will continue to be a compounder of free cash flow over the next 5 years, I just don't like the valuation. Patience is a quality that is investors' best friend.

data.title: Chubb's 20-Year Outperformance Continues As Market Winds Change
data.description:
Chubb (NYSE:CB) is one of the most profitable insurance underwriters around, it has a fortress balance sheet and has grown shareholder value at a rapid pace. This is a high-quality business and it will continue growing. Chubb reminds us of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), the much larger insurance industry major.

The share price of Chubb has gone up by ~45% this year, outpacing invested capital growth. This upward shift, however, can be explained by significant growth in investment income. The rising interest rates have made the equity capital employed by Chubb a lot more profitable.

Even after the strong stock price run this year, we do not see Chubb as overvalued, especially given its long track record of outperformance, which will most likely continue due to the competitive advantages of the company.

We still want to become shareholders of Chubb.

The changing winds in the insurance industry are benefiting Chubb

The Property and Casualty insurance market is, however, cyclical, and the performance has been strong as of late. Strong underwriting performance as well as rising investment income can indeed encourage market participants to reduce prices going forward. Float is now worth a lot more!

There have been periods in the industry where market participants were writing policies at a loss just to be able to get the float and invest it profitably. Even if Chubb can continue outperforming peers, the overall industry pricing changes would still affect the company.

On the flip side, P&C insurance market strength is supported by reduced reinsurance capacity. The reinsurance market was flooded with return-seeking capital when rates were bottoming. A significant share of this capital has left the industry now, reducing CAT reinsurance capacity and pushing up the prices.

Chubb is positioning itself to ride these industry changes from a position of strength. It is increasingly focused on CAT-intensive lines, such as property insurance or high-net-worth personal, that have benefitted from receding reinsurance capacity.

Chubb has one of the strongest balance sheets in the industry and can afford to take on risks that others cannot without the help of reinsurers. It is rather similar to Berkshire Hathaway in this regard. These two insurers keep fortress balance sheets to be able to take on large unique risks that other market participants shy away from.

Chubb is therefore likely to continue growing in the CAT-exposed lines and is also likely to continue writing insurance at industry-leading combined ratios.

Investment income growth will continue driving earnings higher

As of late the fixed-income yields have recovered and for this reason, investment income has grown by 30%+ in 2023. Investment income is going to grow further as long as bonds are reinvested at market rates.

Chubb collects insurance premiums upfront and gets to keep and invest them until losses have to be paid out. The company primarily invests this 'float' in fixed-income securities.

Investment income, or rather, the yields on bonds deliver roughly half of the operating profits of the business. Interest income changes affect the company’s reported earnings to a great degree.

During the second quarter, net investment income reached ~1.5 billion and could approach $6 billion for the full year, accounting for half of the company’s operating profit.

Investment income growth is driven not only by higher yields, however. Invested assets also grow as the insurer writes more premiums. During the first half of the year, net premiums earned increased by 12%. On the other hand, as rates rose, the fixed-income securities were revalued lower.

As rates have stopped increasing now, we would expect the fixed-income investment portfolio value to finally start appreciating as premiums earned continue growing. Invested assets will also get a boost when Chubb makes a significant acquisition.

Over the last 10 years, net written premiums have compounded at ~11%, while the investment income appreciated by 9.5% annually on average.

Chubb - twenty years of outperformance

Despite the significant investment income growth, insurance risk underwriting is still the core expertise and the main shareholder value growth driver of Chubb. For the float to grow sustainably, risks have to be picked wisely.

Insurance is an odd industry. It is easy to grow written premiums, especially when the risk is not priced appropriately or when contracts contain generous long-term provisions. A shady insurance operation can grow seemingly profitably, but tail risks will eventually catch up with them. Underwriting revenue growth could be meaningless in this industry, especially in the large commercial lines.

Commercial insurance is a business of reputation. Customers only want to deal with insurers that are guaranteed to have the financial resources and those that are willing to make good on claims promptly, without any fake obstacles.

The same goes for investing in insurance companies. We should only consider buying operations that have a long-term track record of profitable underwriting and good risk management. Chubb meets these criteria.

Chubb is one of the best Property and Casualty underwriters in the industry. It has exceeded the average underwriting performance over the last 20 years.

Over those 20 years, the company has been steered by the same individual, CEO Evan G. Greenberg. This is one of the longest track records of outperformance we have ever encountered. Mr. Greenberg must have a few tricks up his sleeve, and we are willing to bet that Chubb will continue outperforming under his leadership.

Under Mr. Greenberg’s leadership, Chubb has compounded shareholder returns at 11.4% per annum, outpacing the S&P 500 by 150 points annually on average.

If Mr. Greenberg’s organisation used shortcuts to grow reported earnings in an unsustainable way, these issues would have surfaced by now. Chubb can therefore be trusted.

CAT-exposed P&C lines offer great growth potential

Insurance risk-taking is an art as much as a science. In a lot of segments, risks cannot be easily quantified, for this reason, no technical formula will enable insurance to compete successfully. Experience and the right attitude are as important as sophisticated quantitative models.

The business needs to be willing to walk away from bad risks or when the market pricing is inappropriate, even if this means failing to deliver on growth targets.

The right management approach is crucial for long-term success. I also help to be active in many lines. When the pricing in one segment becomes inappropriate, it is great to have other segments to which management can shift attention.

The balance sheet strength is also very important. As we have already mentioned, large customers prefer to deal with the strongest insurers. On top of this, financial strength opens up profitable segments that are not available to others.

The property and Casualty industry has been struggling with elevated natural catastrophe (CATs) losses, stemming from higher event frequency.

Rising interest rates as well as elevated catastrophe frequency have affected reinsurance prices, which has translated to reduced willingness of the insurers to write CAT-exposed lines. Reinsurance prices are expected to remain high for a few more years.

But the biggest, most diversified and financially the strongest insurers are not as reliant on reinsurance. Chubb is willing to take some of the CAT risks, especially now as the prices are firm.

Chubb is retaining most of its earnings and has accumulated an enviable loss reserve of $80 billion. Even the largest imaginable hurricanes or earthquakes are expected to have only a limited impact on the capital position of the business. The frequency is also well-covered.

Chubb is well positioned to continue writing CAT exposed Property and Casualty insurance lines, while most of the others are reeling from elevated losses of last year and pulling back.

Chubb is performing strongly, and the valuation multiples have now recovered

Chubb is a well-diversified insurance group active across Commercial, Personal and Life insurance lines globally. As of late Life insurance was the fastest growing product group due to rapid expansion in Asia, however, U.S. Commercial and high net-worth Personal are still the main profit centres.

Chubb has delivered strong performance during the second quarter of the financial year. Net Premiums written increased by 12% while investment income grew by 26%. Higher levels of catastrophe losses were incurred in this quarter, however, the overall combined ratio of 86.8% was still strong as compared to industry peers. The core operating income grew by 9.3%.

During the first six months of the year, the core operating income was $10.8 per share. Chubb is expected to earn $21.43 over the full year and currently trades at 14X expected earnings. The actual earnings are subject to changes due to potential catastrophe losses.

Chubb is expected to report third-quarter earnings next week. We expect them to report the underlying combined ratio, excluding CAT losses, higher than the record low of 83.2%, reported last quarter. There has been some talk of firming competition in the property insurance space, and we believe there are some grounds for it. After all, record profits attract competition. Having said that, we would be surprised to see an underlying combined ratio of over 86%.

The catastrophe losses will fluctuate from quarter to quarter. The estimated losses of Hurricanes Helene and Milton are expected to be as high as $60 billion industry-wide. It remains to be seen what losses Chubb will take. Having said that, the capital position of Chubb is strong, and the effect will likely be marginal.

Chubb is currently trading roughly in line with its 10-year average forward earnings. The share price has recovered from the lows of March to July of this year. It would appear that Berkshire Hathaway has taken advantage of the Chubb share price weakness at the beginning of the year to build their position.

There is not much slack in the share price of Chubb currently. The underwriting performance and the investment income are strong, while further valuation multiple upside is limited. It is probably best to wait for those seeking to become Chubb shareholders.

On the other hand, Chubb has been generating attractive returns on equity and expects to continue delivering 13%-14% average RoE going forward. If these expectations are indeed met, long-term holders would be able to generate attractive returns even off the current price.

However, we are somewhat wary of buying insurers at full price. After all, there will always be risks even for the bet insurance operations:

  1. The interest rates could once again return to low levels of before if economic growth is stagnant.
  2. Unexpected liabilities could be from natural disasters or pandemics could reduce the equity base.
  3. Changing reinsurance market cycles might make P&C underwriting more challenging in the future.

At the moment, we prefer Markel (MKL) in the insurance space. The earnings of this business are also anchored by a large investment portfolio, their underwriting performance, however, has not been great as of late. For this reason, Markel trades at significantly lower valuation multiples than Chubb.

The summary

Chubb is a large and diversified insurer that has delivered stellar underwriting performance over the last 20 years, under the leadership of Evan Greenberg.

Most recently, the investment income of the insurer surged higher as interest rates have finally recovered and so did earnings valuation multiples. The stock price has therefore appreciated by 45% over the past year.

Chubb currently seems to be trading in line with historical averages, while the profitability is strong. We believe Chubb is fully valued at the moment.

We will Hold out from buying Chubb now but will continue waiting for an opportunity to invest in this great quality business.

data.title: Unemployment Claims Down 15K, Better Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

In the week ending October 19th, initial jobless claims were at a seasonally adjusted level of 227,000. This represents a decrease of 15,000 from the previous week's figure and is better than forecasts for 243,000.

Here is the opening statement from the Department of Labor:

In the week ending October 19, the advance figure for seasonally adjusted initial claims was 227,000, a decrease of 15,000 from the previous week's revised level. The previous week's level was revised up by 1,000 from 241,000 to 242,000. The 4-week moving average was 238,500, an increase of 2,000 from the previous week's revised average. The previous week's average was revised up by 250 from 236,250 to 236,500.The advance seasonally adjusted insured unemployment rate was 1.3 percent for the week ending October 12, an increase of 0.1 percentage point from the previous week's unrevised rate. The advance number for seasonally adjusted insured unemployment during the week ending October 12 was 1,897,000, an increase of 28,000 from the previous week's revised level. This is the highest level for insured unemployment since November 13, 2021 when it was 1,974,000. The previous week's level was revised up 2,000 from 1,867,000 to 1,869,000. The 4-week moving average was 1,860,750, an increase of 17,500 from the previous week's revised average. The previous week's average was revised up by 500 from 1,842,750 to 1,843,250.

Here is a closer look at the series since 2007, with a callout to the past 12 months. The four-week moving average, which gives a clearer sense of the overall trend, is currently at 238,500. This is an increase of 2,000 from the previous week's figure.

For an even shorter timeline, the chart below shows initial unemployment claims starting in 2022.

As we can see, there's a good bit of volatility in this indicator, which is why the four-week moving average is a more useful number than the weekly data. Here is the complete data series dating back to 1967.

Outside of the COVID spike, initial unemployment claims have never been greater than 700,000 for a given week. With that said, we've adjusted the y-axis on the chart below so that we can get a zoomed in view of the series where the COVID spike isn't as prominent.

Notice the relationship between recessions and the rise in weekly unemployment claims. To no surprise, the 4-week moving average begins to rise at or before the start of a recession and reaches a relative peak at the end of a recession.

The headline unemployment insurance data is seasonally adjusted, as are the charts above. What does the non-seasonally adjusted data look like? See the chart below, which clearly shows the extreme volatility of the non-adjusted data (the green dots). The four-week MA gives an indication of the recurring pattern of seasonal change (note, for example, those regular January spikes).

Again, in this next chart, we've adjusted the y-axis to get a zoomed in view of the series where the COVID spike isn't as prominent.

Because of the extreme volatility of the non-adjusted weekly data, we can add a 52-week moving average to give a better sense of the secular trends. The chart below also has a linear regression through the data (red line), which we are currently below.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: S&P Global: Why The Market Isn't Responding To The Q3 2024 Beat
data.description:
S&P Global (NYSE:SPGI) followed its largest competitor Moody's (MCO) in handily exceeding consensus estimates in the third quarter, driving 16% revenue growth and adjusted EPS growth of 21%.

Despite the better-than-expected results, shares are falling as of market open, and are now down about 5% since Moody's reported.

As I expected in my previous article, the market is worried about the pull-forward of demand from 2025, more than it is happy about the near-term success. There were also some other areas of weakness worth noting.

Let's dive in.

Introduction To S&P Global

S&P Global is a financial services powerhouse, operating in five diversified segments that are intertwined through data, customers, technology, and underlying drivers.

With information tools under Market Intelligence, Commodity Insights, and Mobility, proprietary benchmarks under Indices, and operating one of the two largest rating agencies in the world under Ratings, I view S&P Global as one of the best ways to capitalize on global GDP growth.

In a series of articles covering the company since April 2023, I maintained a bullish stance, initially with a 'Strong Buy' rating, and later on with a 'Buy'.

So far, it seems that we were correct without analyses, as S&P Global beat the market by a good 6 points over this time period, and handily beat several peers like MSCI (MSCI).

Looking ahead, I believe S&P Global is superbly positioned to continue to grow revenues at a market-beating 6%-9% rate, while constantly improving operating and cash flow margins, resulting in 11%-15% EPS growth.

With that, let's dive into the third-quarter results.

Diving Into Third-Quarter Results

Revenues in the third quarter were $3.6 billion, up 16% Y/Y, and $150 million ahead of consensus expectations. Growth was broad-based across all segments, led by Ratings with 36%, and Indices with 18%.

In Ratings, which we'll discuss in further detail later, growth was driven by opportunistic issuance from companies that are exploiting a uniquely narrow spreads environment, as well as an outlook for rate cuts.

Indices benefitted from very strong capital markets, as linked AUM rose by 46%. Mobility and Commodity Insights both grew around 9%, showcasing significant strength despite unusually weak recall market in Mobility.

Lastly, Market Intelligence was the only weak-point, growing a disappointing 6%. Management cited longer sales cycles, and cost-cut focus from end customers that are weighing down on growth.

The strong revenue growth has contributed to meaningful operational leverage across the company, with adjusted operating margins improving 180 bps from the prior year quarter, resulting in 20% adjusted operating profit growth.

Within segments, Market Intelligence was again the notable standout with a 140 bps decline in margins. Outside of that, all segments were either stable or improved, with Ratings the biggest contributor, as segment margins expanded by 500 bps driven by increased utilization of analysts.

On a consolidated level, guidance saw a significant revision upwards, as they are now expecting growth of 12% at the mid-point (previously 9%), adjusted EPS of $15.2 (previously $14.5), and adjusted FCF of $5.2 billion (previously $4.7).

However, on the division level, we note a small narrowing down of targets for Market Intelligence and Mobility, a narrowing up in Commodity Insights, and significant upgrades in Indices and Ratings.

Overall, this was a great quarter, but it did not answer the main questions on investors' minds looking into 2025. Let's get to that now.

The Main Concern Is The Near-To-Mid-Term Normalized Growth Trajectory

In my last article about the company, I discussed the pull-forward risk that's weighing down on shares. Simply put, investors seem to believe much of today's Ratings strength is derived from issuance that should have been done in 2025.

As a result, instead of celebrating the better-than-expected growth this year, the market is getting increasingly scared about significant deceleration next year.

As noted in the graph above, billed issuance saw extreme strength pretty much August of last year, and remains extremely high even when lapping the tougher comps. This, of course, is not sustainable levels.

Over time, issuance is mostly correlated with global GDP, and Ratings revenues typically grow at around a 2x-3x factor to that number. That means that the current outlook for a best-case scenario in 2025 would be mid-single-digit Ratings revenue growth, which is good, but reflects a significant deceleration.

Furthermore, investors are also noting weakness in the Market Intelligence segment, which has been growing at a company-low mid-single-digit pace over the past three years.

This is the largest segment in terms of revenue and second-largest in terms of operating profit, which means it's a big drag on consolidated growth numbers.

This division is suffering from the change in priorities of its end customers, which primarily include banks, financial institutions, asset managers, and insurance firms.

Generally, they are going through a cost-cutting phase, reducing headcounts or at least headcount growth, and looking for efficiencies. Over the long-term, it's highly likely S&P Global will end up benefitting from this trend, because of its expansive product suite that can allow it to become a single or primary vendor.

Until then, this segment will continue to be viewed cautiously by investors.

Valuation & Outlook

S&P Global is famously known to be a secular growth company that operates in cyclical industries. What this means is that just like the market, there will be occasional down cycles, but in the long-term, all its businesses recover quickly, and revert to beating record highs.

This is exactly the case with the Ratings business and Indices segments currently, and this is what I expect to occur with Market Intelligence as well.

When it comes to the pull-forward risk, I find it a bit overexaggerated, as both S&P Global and Moody's have said they're not seeing abnormalities in the 2025 maturity wall.

In the long term, it has significant secular tailwinds including huge refinancing walls in the upcoming years, an uptick in M&A, and growth in private credit.

Overall, I'm firm in my outlook for around 8% revenue growth and 12% EPS growth for the foreseeable future.

Now, when it comes to valuation, here's the deal. This is what I wrote in my last article:

S&P Global is currently trading at 35x forward earnings and 32 times '25 earnings. This is a bit above the company's historical average.

It could have still been attractive if we were ahead of a major upcycle, but not when we're in the later innings of one.

Assuming growth decelerates from ~11% this year to ~8% next year, and EPS grows around 11%, I don't see huge upside from current levels.

Still, I think S&P should be a cornerstone in long-term portfolios, and encourage buying aggressively when the multiple is in the 30x range (around 6% below current levels).

Perhaps not surprisingly, we've arrived at that 30x range I anticipated. Therefore, I'm back to being more enthusiastic about the near term upside.

My price target is $577 a share by late-2025, reflecting about 17% upside in the upcoming 12 months. This is derived from a 31x multiple on 2026 estimates.

Conclusion

S&P Global had an extraordinary third quarter, but counterintuitively, the stronger results are right now, the higher the worries are from deceleration next year.

That said, I believe the pull-forward concerns are overexaggerated, and I expect Market Intelligence weakness to not last for long.

The long-term framework of high-single-digit revenue growth and low-double-digit EPS growth remains intact, which means S&P global is a buy below the historical average valuation of 30 times earnings.

This leads me to reiterate a 'Buy', encouraging investors to capitalize on the recent weakness in the stock.

data.title: Toast: Shaping Up Into A Real Winner
data.description:
I've written about Toast (NYSE:TOST) a few times as one of my favorite speculative stocks to cover. The company bridges the gap between cloud software and fintech payment solutions, while slowly but surely chugging towards dominance in the niche restaurant space.

If you pay attention, the next time you're in a restaurant, it's becoming more and more likely the little console they hand you to close your tab will have a symbol of a piece of toast on top. The company has made good use of both its land and expand model as well as referrals to carve out a sizeable market share.

Surprisingly, one of the major concerns I had going into my initial research of the business is not valid. Toast manages to maintain surprisingly low attrition, despite the notoriously fickle restaurant industry. Historical net revenue retention rates around 121% (from 2020-2023) highlight best-in-class product stickiness and upsell. How could this be?

I think this is part of the company's secret sauce, and it's multifaceted. Part of it is that restaurants actually improve operations when bringing Toast onboard and become less likely to fail. Here are a couple of customer success stories from a recent earnings call, which I always enjoy reading:

Let me share 2 recent customer stories that speak to our momentum and the value we create for customers. Pickles Pub is a busy full-service restaurant and bar in downtown Baltimore near Camden Yards and M&T Bank Stadium. They switched to Toast to partner with a technology partner that could handle their volume, streamline operations and increase speed of service, particularly on those busy game days. On opening day for the Orioles, they blew through their previous sales record and credit the uplift in part to faster speed of service after implementing Toast Go Handhelds, Mobile Order & Pay and Kitchen Display Systems.

Since switching to Toast, they have reduced order to delivery times for drinks to under a minute. And in addition, the addition of Mobile Order & Pay has opened up a new service model with 20% of food and drink orders coming in directly from guests scanning QR codes at the table and placing the orders themselves.

I'm also excited to announce our agreement with Snooze Eatery, a breakfast and lunch concept with over 70 locations nationwide. Snooze needed a technology partner that could support their growth plans and provide a better guest experience with faster and more accurate service. They're rolling out Toast across their footprint. And for the locations they've taken live, they're already seeing benefits. Toast handhelds have increased server capacity, leading to higher sales, higher tips and greater earnings. As these 2 stories highlight, our focus on creating products that drive value for our customers continues to drive our growth.

Another piece of it, though, is the initial contract terms. Customers sign on for 1-3 year initial terms (which validates the company's initial hardware outlay at a loss). Once Toast is integrated into the restaurant's operations, it's exceedingly difficult to switch providers without a real need. With that, Toast isn't extorting outrageous take rates, averaging around 55 bp's of sales.

The last major piece, as I see it, is Toast Capital. The company recently began selling off these loans to third parties (taking a cut as the originator) to defray some risk, and this side of the business will never be the real moneymaker. However, Toast's data stream on company operations and the ability to pay itself back directly from order flow are both huge advantages over a bank in underwriting. Toast knows better than anyone, save the owner of the restaurant the amount the business can support in a small business loan. That restaurant will also have a tough time getting approved elsewhere, potentially, further driving loyalty to the Toast brand.

As I move into talking about some recent results, I always have to put in a disclaimer about the company's Annualized Recurring Run-Rate (ARR). Feel free to read the above from the 10-Q, or I'll summarize it here. Toast has two major revenue streams, software and fintech. Fintech is driven off restaurant sales volume processed on the company's rails, and software is a subscription-based model, very similar to other SaaS businesses. This makes the company somewhat difficult to comp for valuation. The way the company defines ARR makes it sound recurring (it's in the name, right?), but I would by no means consider restaurant food sales to be recurring revenue. Keep that in mind when we talk valuation, because direct comparisons to SaaS companies don't make a lot of sense.

Looking at the recent operating results from the 10-Q above, Toast has taken the turn I've been waiting for. In the most recent quarter, G&A expenses were down YOY (a killer for young tech companies), R&D was slightly down, and with sales growth the actual GAAP operating income was positive. S&M going up isn't necessarily a negative (neither is R&D), but a positive operating income is a huge sign management takes profitability seriously.

Recent results with ARR up 29% were consistent and strong, despite the drawbacks of looking at ARR. Free cash flow grew from $39M to $108M yoy, and GPV grew 26%. With recent wins, I anticipate neutral earnings on Q3. The full-year guidance is mostly in line with these results and I don't have any reason to believe from a macro perspective, Toast will suffer any setbacks.

With that, there's still plenty of runway in American restaurants, and management has shown an ability to keep the growth engine chugging. The company reports initial momentum in UK, Canada, and Ireland, and the company is also trying to work upmarket with an enterprise tier offering in the software. The company has signed on around 2000 partner restaurants internationally, and in the most recent quarter brought on restaurant chains like Uno Pizza and Legal Seafood. Location count grew 29% to 120,000. Moving upmarket will likely be somewhat dilutive to margins, but is the next logical step for the business to drive significant GPV growth. I see this as one of the key risks for the company. As they move upmarket, growth will be harder to come by.

Another risk to consider is the impacts due to recession. While Toast garners a portion of their revenue from stickier software subscription sales, restaurants are not historically resilient businesses during a recession. Not only will they suffer in terms of GPV, plenty of small restaurants go under. That being said, Toast survived COVID, which may have been the worst shock to restaurants in my lifetime. Additionally, it can be spun as a way to convince restaurants to subscribe since Toast helped operations become more seamless for takeout, etc.

As for valuation, it's tough. The company's P/S ratio of 3.77X does not compare favorably to Square's 2X or Shift4's 2.14X. However, those companies' revenues aren't structured the same way. I'm going to do some real dirty back of the napkin math, so bear with me. I see a terminal operating margin in the ballpark of 10-12% here, as gross margins have room to improve and the company scales. Applying 10% OM's to today's revenues would give me a ballpark of 31X earnings. Before you get your pitchforks, let me caveat to say this is all purely academic. The company today is speculative, but I think it's moving in the right direction and I don't mind trying to catch some trains right as they leave the station as part of an overall investment strategy. I'll continue adding to Toast here, it's a buy.

data.title: Starbucks: U.S. And China Cast Shadows To Near-Term Outlook
data.description:
Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX) released its preliminary Q4 and FY2024 results, which showed further weakness in its domestic and international fundamentals. US comp sales were down 6%, and Chinese comp sales were down 14%. The company also suspended its fiscal 2025 outlook in light of the ongoing fundamental challenges.

SBUX now expects FYQ4 comp sales to decline by 7%, driving a consolidated revenue decline of 3% y/y to $9.1bn and a GAAP EPS decline of 25% y/y to $0.80, which are largely in line with consensus.

It is worth recalling that the stock was up more than 20% after SBUX announced the new CEO back in August, which largely drove the YTD share price recovery. With the euphoria around the new CEO gradually dissipating, we believe that investors should shift their focus to the fundamentals of the business, that Starbucks will need time to address the existing challenges and that a brand turnaround strategy is unlikely to materialize in the near term.

SBUX is facing several challenges, both in the US and in China.

The challenges in the US related to constrained consumer spending and poor app experience have been widely documented. This challenge appears to be an ongoing theme, with US comparable transactions expected to decline by 10% this upcoming quarter and average ticket size expected to increase by 4%. The company pointed out that the investment in both products and in-app promotion failed to drive meaningful reacceleration in customer behaviors, particularly in the Starbucks Reward and non-SR segments.

Although improving the app experience by enhancing the wait time precision may address a large part of the consumer experience challenge, this may require a larger improvement in store efficiency and production workflow that may require multiple quarters to implement and adjust, which may partially explain why the company has ceased issuing the 2025 outlook.

Although China remains a small portion of the overall operating profit contribution, the current optic on China is not constructive to SBUX’s current fundamentals. China continues to see a combination of macro and competitive challenges, as implied by the 8% decline in average ticket size (price competition) and 6% decline in comparable transactions (macro weakness and wallet shift to value alternatives).

Luckin Coffee (OTCPK:LKNCY) is perhaps SBUX’s biggest competitor, with over 40% share in both GMV and cups sold on Meituan. However, it is worth noting that Luckin is not the only low-cost competitor that SBUX faces, and other low-cost leaders are driving the deflationary environment in China.

Most notably, Mixue has emerged as the country’s low-cost leader. China’s broad consumption downgrade trend positions Mixue favorably, given that its products, on average, are sold at ~Rmb6/unit for both tea and coffee, which is well below SBUX, which sells its cheapest coffee at around RMB 30-35 and Luckin’s at RMB 10.

Additionally, Mixue’s focus on delivering superior taste to unsophisticated coffee drinkers at an affordable price is particularly attractive, especially in lower-tier cities where consumers place lower emphasis on the nuance of coffee quality (i.e. bean quality, taste etc.) and instead prefer coffee-infused beverages that simply "taste good". This consumer preference positions SBUX unfavorably for China's lower-tier cities, given that it diminishes SBUX's core differentiation relative to competing coffee chains, milk tea and other beverage providers.

Per its Hong Kong prospectus, Mixue generated revenue of RMB 15.4bn for the first nine months of 2023 (+46% y/y), a net profit margin of 15%, and FCF of over RMB 1bn.

Mixue’s core products include fruit drinks, tea, ice cream, and coffee, priced at around $1 or RMB6. The firm has two brands: Mixue (focused on tea) and Lucky Brand (focused on coffee).

The company has come a long way since its early days, when it started with shaved ice and subsequently sold an RMB1 ice cream cone that enhanced Mixue’s brand equity. The fruit drink venture started with lemonade in 2013, followed by coffee (2017).

The company operates under a franchise model that allowed it to quickly scale its footprint to 36k stores in China and 11 overseas locations as of 3Q23, selling 5.8 billion cups during the period.

Although many would argue that Mixue (and even Luckin) does not compete directly against SBUX given their different market positioning largely in the low-end, the value proposition from Mixue is difficult to ignore for many consumers within China’s current macro context.

We note that a typical Americano costs less than $1 (RMB 6), and a latte costs $1 (RMB 7). The beans are sourced from Ethiopia, Columbia, Brazil, and Indonesia, while the roasting plant equipment is from Probat and Buhler. For consumers who would like to get their coffee without an emphasis on brand, Mixue and the low-cost alternatives appear to be the better option.

With the competitive environment in China unlikely to abate any time soon, SBUX remains a “show me” story for the majority of fiscal 2025. Without a visible turnaround in same-store sales in the US and China, the stock could de-rate, and we could see downside risk to current consensus estimates.

SBUX is scheduled to release its full-quarter results on October 30.

data.title: Netflix: Promising Growth Story With Solid Financial Performance, But Overbought Right Now
data.description:
Netflix, Inc. (NASDAQ:NFLX) has released another strong report confirming the growth re-acceleration. The subscriber's amount has grown, prices are on a gradual rise track, and the advertising platform will be launched globally in 2025. These are three key drivers of future sustainable growth. But despite this, the current share price of $749.29 (as of 10/23/24) makes the company quite expensive. It leaves almost no reasonable potential for growth in the short term despite the positive fundamental background, but in the long term the investment can bring reasonable return and this explains my HOLD rating.

Q3 Earnings Results Highlights

Record quarterly revenue, which came close to $10 billion, confirmed the trend towards increasing growth rates in recent periods (15% YoY vs. 12.5% ​​a year earlier) and allowed for a significant leap in operating margin (almost 30% vs. 22% a year earlier).

This brings the net profit margin TTM to the 20% threshold for the first time in history. For me, this is a signal of the company's transition to the "major league". Because it indicates the company's significant dominance in its area since the growing scale allows for savings on costs due to the extremely efficient business model.

I would especially like to outline the cash flow provided by operating activities improvement. My main complaint about Netflix's financial performance previously was the inability to confirm accrued net income with real cash flows since additions to content required too many expenses. This item in the cash flow statements remained at ~$4b ($3.7b to be exact, with $3.6b a year earlier, the same as at the end of Q3 2022), but growing revenue and, as a result, net income with a relatively lower amortization of content value allowed the earnings quality to reach the desired level for me, as a value investor, of 100%.

Since June 2023, earnings quality, which I calculate based on the ratio of net income to cash flow from operations (TTM), has fluctuated around 100%, although before that, it was closer to zero.

Fundamental Prospects For Reaccelerated Growth

Theodore A. Sarandos, CEO, began his earnings call with the phrase:

We had a plan to reaccelerate growth, and we delivered on that plan.

Actual Q3 results were above guidance and allow us to expect a strong finish to FY24 and a good start to next year, where revenue is expected to be at $43-44b (+11-13% YoY).

The consensus for 2029 revenue is $63.5b, which implies a 9.7% CAGR. This rate is in line with current actuals and certainly does not fit the description of "reaccelerated". Moreover, I believe it is already priced.

When asked about investment priorities, the company's management focused on investing in content. The current model works (we can see this by the margins) and should provide organic growth. Gaming, going live (Netflix plans to broadcast live boxing and NFL games in December) and similar initiatives are not game-changers but should provide improvement of AMR (streaming revenue divided by the average number of streaming paid memberships divided by the number of months in the period).

Something more is needed, and that has to be advertising, although management is currently avoiding predictive numbers and is very cautious about expectations for the launch of its ad-tech platform, which will be tested in Canada next month.

Here's why I'm betting on advertising becoming a solid source of cash flow in the long term:

  • Netflix has the lowest share of ad-supported users of any streaming service. As of Q1 2024, the share of add-free subscribers was 78%, which gives the company a huge reserve and potential. Ads membership was up 35% quarter on quarter based on the results of Q3 2024.

  • Netflix's revenue per ad-supported user is about $70, and even despite the predicted decline in this value by 2026, it will remain by far the largest among competitors.

  • The majority of Netflix's audience is made up of Generation Z (68% of Zers surveyed watch Netflix, putting it second only to YouTube). This audience tends to opt for cheaper ad-free plans, fueling the growth of the ads segment. Netflix also boasts high engagement rates and is the most used streaming service by users of other ones. This level of loyalty will help balance subscription revenues and advertising growth.

The company's response to the slowdown in 2022 was timely. Today, the combination of strong financial performance and advertising plans make Netflix the obvious number-one investment idea in the industry for me. And even Disney can't stop it.

Why I Bet on Netflix Over Disney

The Walt Disney Company (DIS) is often cited as a major competitor and even a favorite to battle Netflix, not only in the context of Disney+, ESPN, and Hulu services but also due to its more diversified business than pure streaming. I will use an objective and undeniable quantitative approach to demonstrate why I believe NFLX is a more efficient unit.

To identify competitive advantages and market dominance, I use a quantitative valuation model consisting of 8 key metrics, mostly collected from the income statement. Key comparison metrics such as net profit margin (20% for Netflix vs. 5% for Disney) and ROIC (20% vs. 5%) put NFLX ahead for me. In addition, Netflix's projected revenue CAGR is significantly higher: 9.7% vs. 2.7%.

Main Risks

As a long-time Netflix user, I don't consider the risk of content production and sales to be significant, since the company has created a working system of content production and licensing for every taste and in different regions. This can be confirmed by the qualitative trend of subscriber base growth to almost 283 million people (14.4% YoY in the last quarter).

Since Netflix is ​​currently trading as a growth story, the obvious risk is a miss in the forecasts and the reaccelerating growth model. Although management is reserved in its expectations, the current valuation (43x) suggests that investors are expecting more than the current ~15% YoY. I did not see a single number about ads (in $) in the latest presentation for the upcoming 2025, which personally made me wary ahead of a new phase of business scaling and changes in monetization.

There is also a minor solvency risk. Netflix's total debt is about $16b. This is 0.7 of shareholders` equity and more than 2 times the annual cash flow from operating activities. While interest expenses make up only 7% of operating profit, the debt burden is not so significant, however, as a conservative long-term investor, I keep an eye on solvency metrics.

But the main risk is the company's valuation. And this risk is already being realized today.

Overvalued Relative To Fair Price By 12%

I don't like comparing companies to each other or the sector average. The fact that Netflix is ​​valued at 43x P/E today compared to the sector median of ~13x doesn't mean much to me personally.

However, I agree with the rather low NFLX valuation grade, although from an entirely different perspective.

Since the company passes my test of profitability, financial health, and growth, and I consider it an investment attractive outside the context of its price per share, I evaluate it relative to itself. To achieve this, I only need the earnings data for the last 16 quarters and the closing prices (as of the quarterly report date).

Takeaway

Netflix is ​​taking its business to new heights, providing shareholders with a promising growth story. The HOLD rating on NFLX shares is a combination of the company's overall investment attractiveness and its relatively overvalued current price. At levels above $800, I would consider reducing my current position to manage market risk. In the long term, I believe in the company's future, which is well illustrated by a quote from Theodore A. Sarandos, CEO:

Over $600 billion in consumer spend in the areas and countries that we operate, we're only capturing roughly 6% to 7% of that today. That's a tremendous upside if we can just stay focused on that continuous improvement and drive to that future.

data.title: Avantor: Uninspiring, Even Amidst Some Green Shoots
data.description:
Shares of Avantor (NYSE:AVTR) have been struggling for quite a while. The life science tools provider and partner has seen a stagnant share price for good reasons.

A demanding earnings multiple, elevated debt load and underwhelming growth performance all make that shares have been lagging. While the company sees organic sales declines improve a bit, and it announced a bolt-on divestment over the summer, these modest green shoots are not sufficient to reconsider a cautious stance here given the premium valuation and leverage employed.

Life Science Tools

Avantor is a global partner for laboratory consumables and a material provider for bioprocessing and biomaterials. The company aims to help its pharmaceutical and biopharmaceutical customers with research & discovery, development and scale-up, as well as production.

The company reports and operates two complementary segments. The largest of these is a $4.7 billion laboratory solutions business. This segment offers comprehensive lab solutions for highly regulated industries, involving proprietary products, services & digital solutions, as well as third-party products.

This is complemented by a $2.2 billion bioscience production business. Within this segment the company provides materials for bioprocessing purposes, silicone for healthcare applications, and advanced technologies and materials for semiconductor & defense companies. While this segment is much smaller in terms of its size, it posts superior margins compared to the core laboratory segment.

The company has been a publicly traded business since 2019. With shares trading in the mid-teens at the time, shares rallied to highs in the $40s late in 2021 on the back of the pandemic-related trends. After this tailwind evaporated, shares have been trading range bound between $20 and $30 per share.

These share price movements correlate with the topline sales trends. A $6.0 billion business in 2019 saw sales peak at $7.5 billion in 2022, aided by some pandemic-related trends, with revenues now trending at $6.8 billion per annum. Frankly, growth has been anything but impressive, in all honesty, certainly if we consider that the company acquired Masterflex in a nearly $3 billion deal in 2021, amongst some smaller M&A efforts as well.

The Base Case

In February of this year, Avantor reported its 2023 results. Reported sales fell by just over 7% to $6.97 billion, with organic revenue declines seen at 5% and change on the back of the pandemic tailwinds being on the reverse.

The company posted adjusted EBITDA of $1.31 billion, resulting in an elevated leverage ratio of 3.9 times. On the bottom line, adjusted earnings were reported at $1.06 per share, down substantially from a $1.41 per share number in 2022. GAAP earnings were reported at just $0.47 per share, but as the vast majority of the earnings discrepancy stems from amortization and impairment charges, and not stock-based compensation expenses, I am happy to use the adjusted earnings numbers.

By April, the company posted a 5% and change decline in first quarter sales to $1.68 billion, with adjusted earnings posted at just $0.22 per share. By July, the company posted a 2% fall in second quarter sales to $1.70 billion, as adjusted earnings were posted at $0.25 per share.

Net debt has come down to $4.84 billion, and with EBITDA reported at $589 million in the first half of the year, the company reported a 3.9 times leverage ratio. The 683 million shares of the business granted equity a $15.7 billion valuation at $23 per share, for a $20.5 billion enterprise valuation.

Frankly, these are demanding valuations with adjusted earnings trending around a dollar per share, and the growth track record being rather underwhelming, certainly considering that the company torches along a great deal of leverage here.

A Divestment

In an effort to address leverage, Avantor announced a bolt-on divestment in August. The company announced the sale of the clinical service business in a $650 million deal to Audax Private Equity. These assets include kitting, biorepository and related equipment and ancillaries.

The segment employs about 800 workers and is part of the company's laboratory segment. With a $200 million revenue contribution, the activities are valued at 3.2 times sales, largely in line with the overall valuation of Avantor at large.

However, after-tax cash proceeds were seen at just $475 million which is a bit of a bummer, but will still reduce overall net debt by around 10%, while about 3% of sales will leave the door. The deal closed in October, as Avantor actually obtained $500 million in after-tax proceeds, a bit more than originally guided for.

What Now?

The latest 2024 outlook calls for sales at a midpoint of around $6.95 billion, with adjusted earnings seen around a dollar per share. This is of course ahead of the latest divestment, which will reduce sales a bit, but likely hardly has an impact on earnings here (as interest expenses will be forfeited as well).

With the company trading at a low ~20 times adjusted earnings multiple and leverage still being high, I am put off by the combination of this, certainly in light of the lagging past performance. While the latest divestment helping a bit to decrease leverage, the overall risk-reward is simply not compelling enough to consider an allocation here.
